title: File Context Managers and JSON
subtitle: Auto-close files safely and work with JSON data
learning_objectives: Use a with statement as a context manager to open and auto-close files
Iterate over lines in a file using a for loop inside a with block
Convert a Python dictionary to a JSON string with json.dumps()
Parse a JSON string back into a Python dictionary with json.loads()

sections.heading: The with Statement for Files
sections.content:
In the last lesson you always had to remember to call .close(). Python provides a cleaner solution: the with statement (also called a context manager). When the indented block under with finishes — even if an error occurs — Python automatically closes the file for you.

The pattern is:
with open("file.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()

After the block, f is already closed. You never need to call f.close() manually.
sections.code_example:
# Reading with a context manager
with open("sample.txt", "r") as f:
    content = f.read()

print(content)
# f is now closed automatically

# Iterating line by line
with open("names.txt", "r") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())
sections.code_output:
Hello from sample.txt!
This is line two.
This is line three.

Alice
Bob
Carol
sections.note: Iterating directly over a file object (for line in f:) is memory-efficient because Python reads one line at a time instead of loading the whole file.

sections.heading: Writing Files with a Context Manager
sections.content: The with statement works for writing too. Opening in "w" mode inside a with block is now the recommended style because it guarantees the data is flushed and the file is closed even if an exception occurs mid-write.
sections.code_example:
lines_to_write = ["Line one", "Line two", "Line three"]

with open("output.txt", "w") as f:
    for line in lines_to_write:
        f.write(line + "\n")

# Read it back to verify
with open("output.txt", "r") as f:
    print(f.read())
sections.code_output:
Line one
Line two
Line three

sections.heading: Working with JSON
sections.content:
JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is the most common format for exchanging data on the web. Python's built-in json module converts between Python objects and JSON strings.

  • json.dumps(obj) — dump to string — converts a Python dict/list to a JSON-formatted string
  • json.loads(string) — load from string — parses a JSON string and returns a Python dict/list

The s in dumps and loads stands for string. There are also json.dump() and json.load() (without the s) that read/write directly to file objects.
sections.code_example:
import json

# Python dict -> JSON string
person = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30, "active": True}
json_string = json.dumps(person)
print(json_string)
print(type(json_string))

# JSON string -> Python dict
data = json.loads(json_string)
print(data["name"])
print(type(data))
sections.note: Notice that Python's True becomes JSON's true (lowercase). JSON uses lowercase true/false/null while Python uses True/False/None.

exercises.title: Read with Context Manager
exercises.prompt: Use a with statement to open sample.txt and print each line stripped of its trailing whitespace and newline. Do not call .close() manually.
exercises.starter_code:
# Use a with block to open sample.txt
# Iterate line by line and print each line stripped of trailing whitespace
hints: Iterate directly over f: for line in f:
Use line.strip() inside the print() to remove the trailing newline.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The contents of sample.txt should be printed.

exercises.title: Write with Context Manager
exercises.prompt: Use a with statement to write a file called cities.txt containing three lines: "London", "Tokyo", and "Cairo". Then open it again with another with block and print all its contents.
exercises.starter_code:
# Write London, Tokyo, Cairo to cities.txt using a with block
# Then open it again with a with block and print all contents
exercises.solution:
with open("cities.txt", "w") as f:
    f.write("London\n")
    f.write("Tokyo\n")
    f.write("Cairo\n")

with open("cities.txt", "r") as f:
    print(f.read())
hints: Call f.write() three times, each with \n at the end.
No need for f.close() — the with block handles it.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
London should appear in the output.
Tokyo should appear in the output.
Cairo should appear in the output.

exercises.title: Parse a JSON String
exercises.prompt: Import the json module. The variable json_str is a JSON string. Use json.loads() to parse it into a Python dictionary called data, then print the value associated with the key "city".
exercises.starter_code:
import json

json_str = '{"city": "Paris", "population": 2161000}'
# Create a variable called data by parsing json_str with json.loads()
# Print the value of the "city" key
exercises.solution:
import json

json_str = '{"city": "Paris", "population": 2161000}'
data = json.loads(json_str)
print(data["city"])
hints: Use json.loads(json_str) to convert the string to a dict.
Then access data["city"] just like any dictionary.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.
The city value 'Paris' should be printed.

quiz.question: What is the main advantage of using 'with open(...) as f:' over manually calling f.close()?
options: It automatically closes the file even if an exception occurs inside the block
It is required for writing files
quiz.explanation: The with statement guarantees the file is closed when the block exits, whether normally or due to an exception. Forgetting f.close() can leave files locked or cause data loss.

quiz.question: What does json.dumps({"x": 1}) return?
options: The dictionary {"x": 1} written to a file
quiz.explanation: json.dumps() converts a Python object to a JSON-formatted string. The result is a string, not a file and not the original dict.

completion_message: Outstanding! Using with for file handling is the modern Python standard, and JSON is the language of the web. You now have the core file and data skills used in countless real projects.
